title: Chapter 25: Modern Law
content: Three hours and forty-seven minutes.

That's how long it took Evelyn to file a federal lawsuit against a demon. Personal record, she said, like she was talking about a 5K. Like this was just another motion in just another case and not — you know — the first time anyone has sued Hell in the Southern District of New York.
Case number: 1:26-cv-04721.

I stare at it on my laptop screen, the glow sharp in the dark of Leo's apartment. Every digit means something. The 1 for the first division. The 26 for the year. The 04721 — sequential, mundane, a number in a queue alongside patent disputes and securities fraud. Plaintiffs v. Defendants. Chloe Hart v. Mephisto MCN, LLC. Six counts.

Evelyn's voice crackles through the phone on speaker, clipped and surgical. She's reading me the filing, paragraph by paragraph, and I can hear the edges of the words sharpen as she goes — the way a blade sounds when it's being honed.

"Count One," she says. "Plaintiff alleges that Defendant's contract constitutes an unlawful instrument of data extraction, identity modification, and commercial exploitation in violation of federal statutes enacted subsequent to the contract's governing framework."
I swallow. Cold espresso, hours old, bitter on my tongue. "In English, Ev."

"The contract is illegal under modern law." A pause. The faint click of her pen — she still takes notes by hand, old-school, like the dinosaurs in the firm who remember paper. "Every federal statute passed after the contract was executed — CFAA, BIPA, the AI Act provisions — none of them existed when Mephisto wrote his terms. He's operating under a legal framework that predates the laws that now govern it. That's the flaw, Chloe. The contract is ancient. The law isn't."
The law isn't. Something about that settles in my chest, warm and unfamiliar. Hope, maybe. Or just the caffeine hitting.

Leo's on mute — I can see his icon, the little microphone with the line through it, hovering in the call. He's monitoring, recording, building the technical evidence log. But I know he's listening. I know because when Evelyn says something particularly vicious, his eyes flicker to mine and there's this flash — not quite a smile, but the skeleton of one.

Evelyn keeps reading. Counts Two through Six: fraud in the inducement, unconscionable adhesion contract, violations of the Stored Communications Act, unauthorized biometric data harvesting, and — my personal favorite — conversion. That's the legal term for stealing someone's property and making it yours. In this case, the property is my capacity for love.
Six counts. Six blades.
"Count Six is the long shot," Evelyn says. "Conversion of an intangible. Courts hate it. But I wanted it on record."
"Why?"

"Because if we lose on jurisdiction and this ends up in his court instead of ours, I want every argument preserved for appeal. You don't win a war with one battle. You win it by making sure the enemy has to fight every single engagement."

I don't say anything. My artist's brain is doing the thing it does — seeing the shape of it. The architecture. Evelyn isn't just filing a motion. She's building a cathedral. Every count is a flying buttress, every argument a keystoned arch, and the whole thing rises toward a single spire: the contract is unenforceable because the world moved on and the devil didn't.

"I need to talk about service," Evelyn says, and her tone shifts — still clipped, but with something underneath. Satisfaction? Anticipation? The way a surgeon sounds right before they make the first cut. "We've filed. But filing isn't enough. We need to serve Mephisto MCN formally. Hand the documents to the defendant."
"Great," I say. "Do we mail it to the Seventh Circle? Is there a zip code for that, or—"
"We serve the app."
I blink. "The app."

"The app," she repeats. "Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 4, a business entity can be served through its registered agent or through its primary means of communication with the plaintiff. Every notification Mephisto has ever sent you comes through that app. Technically, functionally, legally — every push alert, every timer update, every system message is a business communication from a registered entity to a contractual party."
Leo unmutes. "You're saying we serve him through his own infrastructure."

"Response-serve," Evelyn says. "When the app delivers a notification, our reply constitutes formal service. The channel already exists. The communication framework is already established. We just have to use it."
"It's never been done before," Leo says. Not a question.

"No. But it's technically valid." And there it is — that edge in her voice. The one that says she's already analyzed this from every angle and she knows it holds. Evelyn doesn't gamble. She plays the percentages with the cold precision of a card counter at a blackjack table. "The rules say what they say. Nowhere does it specify that service must be physical. Nowhere does it exclude digital communication. The standard is reasonable notice, and there is nothing more reasonable than using the channel the defendant itself established and maintains."

Leo is already nodding, already thinking. I can see it — the way his fingers twitch toward his keyboard, the way his eyes go distant for half a second before snapping back. "I can embed the service documents in the metadata of a notification response," he says. "Looks like a regular user acknowledgment on the surface. But the metadata layer contains the full filing — case number, court, counts, summons. Everything."
"And when the system reads the notification," Evelyn says.

"It reads everything," I finish. I know this. I've seen it. The app doesn't just deliver notifications — it ingests them. Every response, every tap, every microsecond of engagement is data. Mephisto's system is a mouth that never stops eating. "He can't not read it."
"Exactly," Evelyn says. "The digital equivalent of handing a subpoena to someone who can't stop opening their mail."

The ozone smell hits me before I see anything — that sharp, metallic tang of electronics running too hot. Leo's been pushing his setup hard. Three monitors, two servers, enough processing power to small-scale mine cryptocurrency, all of it running analysis on the Mephisto MCN app for the past six hours. The air around his desk is warm, almost feverish.

He codes for forty-three minutes. I watch over his shoulder, not understanding most of it — the syntax is a language I don't speak, Python and Swift and something he keeps calling "the ugly part" — but I understand the shape. The payload wrapped in a greeting card. A Trojan horse made of legal documents.
"Ready," he says.
My phone sits on the desk between us. The app is open. The notification badge is a red circle with a number that keeps climbing — new followers, new messages, new everything. The bots never sleep.
"Send it," Evelyn says from the speaker.
Leo hits enter.
For a moment, nothing. The phone sits there, screen bright, the app static. I'm about to ask if it worked—
Then my phone erupts.

Not a notification. An error. The screen flickers — once, twice, a stuttering flash that makes my eyes water. The app's interface fractures, UI elements sliding off their moorings like tiles in an earthquake. The Mephisto MCN logo dissolves into static. And then—
Source code.

Raw. Unfiltered. Scrolling across my phone screen in a cascade of text too fast to read, dense and alien and deeply, viscerally wrong. It's like seeing someone's skeleton through their skin. The architecture of the thing that's been living in my pocket, drinking my data, counting down my love — exposed. Every function, every variable, every nested loop of whatever passes for Mephisto's digital DNA, scrolling and scrolling and—
"Leo," I say.

"I see it." He's recording. I don't even know when he started — his phone is propped on a stand, camera pointed at my screen, capturing every frame. His other hand is taking screenshots at machine-gun speed. "I see all of it."

Thirty seconds. That's how long the source code scrolls. Thirty seconds that feel like thirty minutes, the phone hot in my hand — physically hot, the battery laboring under whatever's happening inside. I can feel the heat through the case, pressing into my palm like a living thing. And the smell — that ozone sharpening, electric, the scent of something burning that isn't quite fire.
Then the app resets.
Clean. Smooth. The interface reassembles itself, tile by tile, like nothing happened. The logo reappears. The notification badge updates. And a new message slides in from the top:
**[Counsel has been retained. Defendant acknowledges service. Hearing scheduled: 72 hours. Forum: Infernal Court, Seventh Circle.]**

I read it twice. Then a third time, because my brain is trying to parse the impossible grammar of it — a formal legal acknowledgment from a demon, delivered via push notification, with the audacity to specify his preferred venue.
"He accepted service," I say.

"He had to," Evelyn says. "That's the beauty of it. His system reads every notification. By reading it, he acknowledged receipt. The acknowledgment is automated — which means it's binding even if he didn't personally see it in the first three seconds. He's been served, and his own infrastructure confirmed it."
"He's demanding the hearing be in his court," Leo says. "Infernal Court. Seventh Circle."

Evelyn makes a sound — not quite a laugh, but the dry exhalation that passes for one in her vocabulary. "He can demand whatever forum he wants. The motion I filed is in federal court. Article III. The Southern District of New York. Under the doctrine of forum non conveniens, we argue that the Infernal Court is a fictitious jurisdiction with no standing under U.S. law."
"A fictitious jurisdiction," I repeat. The words taste like science fiction. Like something you'd read in a novel, not file in an actual courthouse.

"The Southern District has jurisdiction," Evelyn continues, and her voice is steady now, the clipped precision giving way to something harder — conviction. "The plaintiff is a U.S. citizen. The harm occurred on U.S. soil. The defendant operates through U.S.-based digital infrastructure — servers, ISP nodes, app store distribution. Every touchpoint is domestic. Every injury is local. There is no reason — no legal reason — for this case to be heard anywhere except where the damage was done."

She pauses. When she speaks again, there's a quality to her voice I haven't heard before. Not warmth — Evelyn doesn't do warmth, not in the traditional sense. But something adjacent to it. Something that might be the closest she gets to tenderness.

"The judge will either agree with us or he won't. But here's the thing about federal judges, Chloe — they don't like being told that a contract supersedes their jurisdiction. They don't like being told that some other court, some other authority, has the final say over a dispute involving a U.S. citizen on U.S. soil. Ego is a powerful legal tool."

I almost laugh. Almost. Because she's right, and it's absurd, and it might work. The whole strategy hinges on the fact that judges are human beings with human vanity, and no Article III appointee is going to let a jurisdiction from the hereafter poach a case that landed on their docket.
"You're counting on a judge being petty," I say.
"I'm counting on a judge being a judge," she corrects. "Same thing."

I look at the filing on my screen. The case number. The counts. My name — Chloe Hart — above the v., above Mephisto MCN, LLC. A federal case against a demon. It's absurd. It's unprecedented. It might actually work.
But.

The follower count pulses in the corner of the app, red and relentless. 48.9 million. The bots are still flooding in — I can see the numbers ticking upward in real time, a hundred new followers every few seconds, a river of fake engagement pouring into the reservoir that's going to drown what's left of my soul.

I do the math. At the current rate — and it's accelerating, the Accelerant Protocol feeding on itself — I hit 49 million in roughly six hours. The love deduction processes at 49 million. That's the threshold. That's the number Mephisto wrote into the contract, the fine print I didn't read, the clause that turns my follower count into a countdown.

The filing buys me a hearing. But it doesn't buy me a stay. The deduction will process before the hearing. Six hours from now, something is going to reach into my chest and take the last coherent piece of me — and no court order has been issued to stop it.
Leo sees it on my face. Or maybe he sees the numbers. He turns to the phone.
"Evelyn," he says. "Can we get a temporary restraining order? Stop the deduction before it processes?"
A pause. Not the confident, surgical silence of someone assembling an argument. The uncertain silence of someone running the calculus and not liking the numbers.

"I can try," Evelyn says. "But we'd need to demonstrate irreparable harm. And the harm has to be specific — not theoretical. The court needs to see that the injury is actual, imminent, and can't be undone by money damages."
"Loss of the ability to love," Leo says. "That sounds pretty irreparable."

"It sounds theoretical," Evelyn counters. "A judge needs more than a philosophical description. We need evidence. Medical testimony. Neurological scans. Something that shows the court — in concrete, measurable terms — what happens to Chloe Hart when that deduction processes."
My voice comes out flat. Emptier than I intended. "What's more specific than losing the ability to love?"
The silence that follows is not an answer.
It's the space where an answer should be.
